skill.name: understanding-db-schema
skill.description: Explains Logseq DB schema concepts, Datascript attributes, built-in classes, property types, entity relationships, and schema validation patterns.
skill.summary: Understand and design Logseq DB schemas with Datascript and built-in class guidance.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious behavior. Static findings for weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and command execution are false positives caused by Logseq schema terms, Markdown code formatting, and documentation examples; the only remaining low-risk issue is external documentation links.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill references three public Logseq documentation URLs. These are user-facing resource links, not automatic exfiltration or hidden network calls, but they require normal caution when following external sources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain explicit HTTPS links to Logseq documentation resources. No evidence found that the skill automatically sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Examples
low_findings.description: The command-execution detections are Markdown inline code, fenced Clojure examples, and Logseq identifiers. No shell execution directive, script, or dynamic command invocation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected backticks delimit documentation examples and identifiers. The skill contains no executable Ruby, shell, or command runner code at these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Schema Terms Matched As Security Patterns
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography and reconnaissance detections are ordinary schema words, property names, and examples such as Description, internal-id, and class references. No cryptographic operation or reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines are static documentation or data templates. No evidence found of weak hash algorithms, host discovery, scanning, or data collection.

content.value_statement: Logseq DB schemas can be hard to inspect because classes, properties, and Datascript attributes interact across the graph. This skill explains those relationships and gives practical schema patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users.
actual_capabilities: Explains Logseq Datascript EAV concepts and core attribute types.
Maps built-in classes such as Page, Tag, Property, Task, Query, Asset, and Template.
Describes property types, cardinality options, closed values, and UI-related property keys.
Shows how pages, blocks, tags, references, parents, and schema classes relate in the graph.
Provides examples for custom classes and typed properties using Logseq DB conventions.
Points users to public Logseq DB documentation for deeper reference.
limitations: It is reference guidance, not a live database inspector.
It does not validate a real graph unless the user provides schema data.
It may require updates when Logseq changes DB schema behavior.
It does not execute migrations or modify Logseq files.

use_cases.title: Design Custom Logseq Classes
use_cases.description: Plan class inheritance, schema properties, and property cardinality before changing a graph.

use_cases.title: Debug Schema Questions
use_cases.description: Understand built-in attributes and relationships when building tools around Logseq DB graphs.

use_cases.title: Document Graph Architecture
use_cases.description: Create clear explanations of how pages, blocks, tags, and properties are modeled.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Schema Concept
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner question about Logseq DB terminology.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Logseq DB uses Datascript attributes, entities, and references. Keep the explanation practical and include common examples.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Property Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Choosing the right property type for a graph design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Logseq property types for this use case: [describe use case]. Recommend type, cardinality, and closed values when useful.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Class Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Checking a proposed custom class model.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Logseq class and property design: [paste design]. Identify relationship issues, missing properties, and better schema choices.

prompt_templates.title: Model a Complex Graph
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture planning for connected classes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Logseq DB schema for [domain]. Include classes, properties, inheritance, references, and validation concerns.

output_examples.input: What is the difference between a Page and a Tag in Logseq DB?
output_examples.output: A Page is a node used for named content. A Tag is also an entity, but it defines a class that can be applied to other nodes. The same graph can use tags to create typed pages and blocks.

output_examples.input: I need a rating field for books. Which property type should I use?
output_examples.output: Use a number property with single cardinality. Add closed values when the rating scale is fixed, such as one through five. Attach it to a Book class through schema classes.

output_examples.input: How should I represent related research notes?
output_examples.output: Use a node reference property with many cardinality. This lets one note point to several pages or blocks while preserving graph relationships for queries.

best_practices: Start with built-in classes and extend them only when the domain needs a distinct type.
Choose cardinality before adding data so query behavior stays predictable.
Use closed values for controlled vocabularies such as status, priority, or rating.
anti_patterns: Do not use free-text properties when a controlled value list is required.
Do not model every relationship as plain text when node references are needed.
Do not assume page names are globally unique without considering tag combinations.

faq.question: Does this skill inspect my Logseq graph automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides schema guidance and can analyze data only when you provide the relevant content.

faq.question: Can it help create custom classes?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains class inheritance, schema properties, and examples for user-defined classes.

faq.question: Does it support Datascript query design?
faq.answer: It can explain schema relationships that inform queries. It is not a full query optimization tool.

faq.question: Can it validate property types?
faq.answer: It can reason about type choices and common validation rules. It does not run Logseq validation by itself.

faq.question: Is it useful for plugin development?
faq.answer: Yes. Plugin developers can use it to understand built-in classes, properties, and graph relationships.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The skill includes public documentation links and allows web tools for reference lookup. Core guidance is contained in local files.
